topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a relentless soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I stared at the screen, the spreadsheet detailing quarterly bonuses, and my stomach tightened into a knot. Sarah, who had just received her performance review, had been lauded for her contributions, and the accompanying figures made my jaw clench. I glanced over at Mark, the junior analyst who had been praised for his recent project turnaround. His salary bracket was noticeably higher than mine. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic feeling suddenly heavy and cold in my hand. I needed to focus, but my gaze kept drifting back to Mark’s name in the directory.

[story 2]

"Another late night, Alex?" Brenda asked, leaning over my cubicle wall. I forced a bright, brittle smile. "Just finalizing the projections." Inside, a strange churning started in my gut, a restless energy that wouldn't settle. I was reviewing the departmental budget when I saw the figures for the new hires. Mark’s entry stood out, a stark contrast to my own modest compensation package. I chewed on my lower lip, the taste metallic. When Mark casually mentioned a recent company retreat, his tone was light, breezy, and utterly unconcerned with the financial realities that were currently occupying my mind. I quickly steered the conversation toward something safe, my voice sounding oddly strained.

[story 3]

I spent the afternoon meticulously re-checking my own expense reports, a pointless exercise designed to distract me from the numbers staring back at me. Every line item seemed to magnify the disparity. When I finally pulled up the official compensation structure, the difference between my pay and that of the newest team member was glaringly obvious. A deep sigh escaped me, a sound too heavy for the quiet office. I leaned back in my chair, letting my shoulders slump slightly, a physical manifestation of the sudden deflation. I watched Mark across the aisle as he laughed at something his teammate said—a sound that seemed effortlessly buoyant, completely unburdened by the kind of careful calculation that defined my daily existence.
